title: Financial efficiency ratios
summary: 9609 A Level — asset turnover, inventory days, receivables days, and working capital efficiency.

simpleExplanation.title: How fast does capital turn?
simpleExplanation.summary: Efficiency ratios show how well the business uses assets and working capital — faster turnover often means less cash tied up.
steps: Receivables days = trade receivables ÷ credit sales × 365.
Lower days usually mean faster cash conversion.

flashcards.back: Stock building up — slow sales, over-ordering, obsolete stock.
Customers paying slower — credit control weakness or customer cash problems.
Generating more sales per $ of capital — efficient (compare with industry).
Inventory days + Receivables days − Payables days — time cash is tied up.

sections.content: **Financial efficiency ratios** (10.2.3) link **operations** to **finance**. Examiners love **days** calculations — show **÷365** clearly and state whether **higher or lower is better** for each ratio.

Asset turnover = $\frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Capital employed}}$

Inventory days = $\frac{\text{Inventory}}{\text{Cost of sales}} \times 365$

Receivables days = $\frac{\text{Trade receivables}}{\text{Credit sales}} \times 365$

sections.question:
Inventory $45 000; cost of sales $328 000; trade receivables $62 000; credit sales $492 000.

Calculate inventory days and receivables days.
sections.solution:
**Inventory days** = (45 000 ÷ 328 000) × 365 = **50 days** (approx.)

**Receivables days** = (62 000 ÷ 492 000) × 365 = **46 days** (approx.)

On average, inventory is held **50 days** before sale; credit customers pay **46 days** after sale — compare to prior year or industry for interpretation.

items: **Lower inventory days** — fresher stock, less storage cost (but too low risks stock-outs).
**Lower receivables days** — faster cash collection, better credit control.
**Asset turnover** — compare with sector; capital-intensive firms have lower turnover.
